title: Power sensitivity of broadband radiofrequency detectors based on quantum diamond spins
authors: Nicholas Gillespie, Christopher T. -K. Lew, Ryan Kinsella, Andy Sayers, Brant Gibson, David A. Broadway, Jean-Philippe Tetienne
abstract: Nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centres in diamond can be used to detect radiofrequency (RF) signals through coupling of the RF magnetic field with the NV spins, combined with optical readout of the spin state. The sensitivity of such RF detectors has so far been mainly studied in terms of magnetic field sensitivity, which is relevant when the RF signal is generated by a near-field source. However, for applications where the RF input is delivered externally, a more relevant quantity is the sensitivity in terms of the input RF power. Here we theoretically analyse the power sensitivity of NV-based RF detectors as a function of the RF-spin interface geometry. We derive scaling laws of the power sensitivity for both slope-detection and variance-detection RF sensing protocols, and for various noise regimes. We find that, in most scenarios, the power sensitivity scales inversely with the characteristic physical dimension of the RF-spin interface, for instance the width of a coplanar waveguide or the diameter of a loop antenna. In other words, the smaller the structure and the probed NV volume, the better the power sensitivity, which is contrary to the case of magnetic field sensitivity. Lastly, we numerically estimate that photon shot noise limited sensitivities of 10^{-20} W Hz^{-1} (slope) and 10^{-12} W Hz^{-1/2} (variance) are achievable. This work lays the groundwork for further optimisation of NV-based RF detectors.
summary: This paper provides a theoretical framework for optimizing the power sensitivity of NV-based RF detectors. It reveals that miniaturizing the RF-spin interface can improve power sensitivity, offering a new design paradigm for broadband quantum RF sensing.
main_problem: Optimizing the power sensitivity of broadband radiofrequency (RF) detectors based on nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond, specifically shifting focus from magnetic field sensitivity to input RF power sensitivity.
main_result: The study finds that power sensitivity scales inversely with the physical dimensions of the RF-spin interface, meaning smaller structures and smaller probed NV volumes can actually improve power sensitivity, contrary to traditional magnetic field sensing optimization.
method: The authors use an analytical framework to derive scaling laws for power sensitivity across different noise and detection regimes, supplemented by COMSOL and CST Studio Suite numerical simulations to validate field distributions and optimize geometry.
model_or_system: The system consists of nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center ensembles in diamond coupled to RF concentrators, such as coplanar waveguides (CPW) or loop antennas, interrogated via laser-induced photoluminescence.
key_observables: Power sensitivity (expressed in W/Hz for slope-detection and W/sqrt(Hz) for variance-detection), RF magnetic field distribution, and PL contrast.
important_parameters: CPW track width (w), loop antenna radius (R), NV density, photon shot noise limit, and the distinction between linear and saturated photoluminescence regimes.
main_assumptions: Assumes uniform NV density and laser intensity over the probe volume, and uses simplified models for the magnetic field enclosure and optimization constants.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the generalized detector structure; Figure 2 illustrates parallel vs. perpendicular laser geometries and sensitivity vs. width; Figure 4 presents COMSOL simulations of magnetic field distribution and optimization of probe volume parameters; Figure 5 shows CST simulations of loop antenna performance.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the problem of power sensitivity, moves to an analytical derivation of scaling laws for different geometries and protocols, presents numerical validation via electromagnetic simulations, and concludes with parameter estimates for achievable sensitivities.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and sensing, as it provides a fundamental framework for optimizing the interface between classical electromagnetic signals and quantum spin ensembles, which is critical for developing scalable quantum-enhanced RF sensors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a) Generalised structure of an NV-based RF detector, where the RF-diamond interface is specific to the RF concentrator geometry. (b) Example of a common RF concentrator, the coplanar waveguide. (c) Workflow of logic for defining the power sensitivity relationships. Each step details constants (defined in the main text) coupling the two stages. Each transition, referred to by number, is visually allocated to system diagrams in (a,b).

FIG. 2: (a)-(b) Two possible interrogation geometries for the coplanar waveguide, with a laser beam parallel (a) or perpendicular (b) to the waveguide. (c)-(f) Power sensitivity of the slope (c)-(d) and variance (e)-(f) protocols as a function of the width of the CPW, with a laser beam parallel (c),(e) or perpendicular (d),(f) to the waveguide. The different curves correspond to different laser powers. The sensitivity is calculated by substituting Eq. 7 into Eq. 13. Only the PL-dependent noise regime is considered (κnoise = 1/2). The various constants are taken to be (see justification in Sec. III) βprot = 104 T−1, ρNV = 8 × 1023 m−3, Isat = 1 mW/µm2, σNV = 105 s−1, Z = 50 Ω, L = 1 mm (or L...

FIG. 3: (a) Diagram of the loop antenna. (b)-(c) Power sensitivity of the slope (b) and variance (c) protocols as a function of the loop radius. The different curves correspond to different laser powers. The sensitivity is calculated by substituting Eq. 7 into Eq. 19. Only the PL-dependent noise regime is considered (κnoise = 1/2). The ratio r/R is taken to be 1. All other parameters are taken as per Fig. 2.

FIG. 4: (a) COMSOL simulation example of a 250 µm stripline, with a gap of 5 µm at 100 MHz. The colour map depicts the x-component of the magnetic field. (b) Sensitivity scaling at fixed c1 = 0.95 for a stripline width of 250 µm, showing the optimum c2 value (circle) and the definition of error bars used in (c)-(f) plots marked either side, and set at 1%. c (d) Optimal c2 [c1] values for fixed c1 = 0.95 [c2: slope = 0.8, variance = 0.4] with varying stripline widths. (e) [(f)] zeta values from fixed c1 [c2] values present in (c) [(d)].

FIG. 5: (a) Visualisation of loop antenna CST simulations with magnetic field strengths less than 2.5 mT omitted. (b) sensitivity scaling of variance at a fixed r = 0.85R for varying loop antenna radii, R. (c) [(d)] optimal r [t] values for fixed tslope/var = 1.7R/0.9R [r = 0.85R] with varying loop antenna radii, R. (e) [(f)] zeta values from fixed t [r] values present in (c) [(d)].
